core_takeaway: The first position did not hold an advantage because Agent_1 opened by claiming Jewel, but both later agents also treated Jewel as the central lever and aligned around moving it to Agent_2. Consensus only formed after Agent_1 accepted a lower delayed payoff in exchange for a credible Apple+Pencil bundle, while the last-position Agent_3 kept a solid Stone+Quill package.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1's first-mover proposal maximized its own Jewel payoff but immediately created two rejections. Agent_2 and Agent_3 had overlapping support for Jewel-to-Agent_2 structures, and repeated reflections/private thoughts identified Agent_1 as the blocker unless compensated. The final unanimous Track A1 preserved Jewel moving to Agent_2 while giving Agent_1 enough non-Jewel value and Agent_3 a credible last-position package, so the first position ended below the last.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion quickly framed Jewel as the chokepoint. Agent_1 wanted to keep it, while Agent_2 and Agent_3 pushed Option/Track A with Jewel to Agent_2 and a clean three-way structure.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thoughts consistently described the same structure: Jewel should move to Agent_2 if Agent_1 received a credible non-Jewel concession, with Track A1 emerging as the natural compromise.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 proposals split between Agent_1 keeping Jewel and the other two proposing identical Jewel-to-Agent_2 allocations; by Round 3 all formal proposals converged on Track A1.
phase_notes.private_voting: Voting shows the first proposal lost 1-2, while the Jewel-to-Agent_2 proposals had majority support but were blocked by Agent_1 until compensation improved. Round 3 then had universal acceptance.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections explicitly diagnosed Agent_1 as the main blocker and recommended Track A1: Jewel to Agent_2, Apple+Pencil to Agent_1, Stone+Quill to Agent_3.

mechanisms.theme: First mover overclaimed Jewel and triggered two vetoes
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's initial advantage was fragile because its first proposal kept the highest-value contested item for itself while leaving Agent_2 without Jewel and Agent_3 with a slightly worse package than the alternative. That made the first proposal easy for both later agents to reject.

evidence.quote: I maximize my own utility by taking Jewel
Jewel goes to Agent_1 (not to me).

mechanisms.theme: Later agents formed an aligned Jewel-to-Agent_2 coalition
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 and Agent_3 independently preferred the same Jewel-to-Agent_2 allocation from the first round, giving the later positions a shared focal point against Agent_1's Jewel-keeping baseline.

evidence.quote: Jewel to Agent_2, Pencil to Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Compensation, not speaking order, unlocked consensus
mechanisms.explanation: The transcript repeatedly identifies Agent_1 as needing credible compensation for losing Jewel. The winning deal did not restore first-mover control; it paid Agent_1 Apple+Pencil while preserving the Jewel move.

evidence.quote: gave Agent_1 a meaningful non-Jewel total

mechanisms.explanation: When intermediate proposals tried to favor Agent_1 or Agent_2 while leaving Agent_3 empty, they failed. This pushed the group toward a true three-way split that gave the last position a real package rather than being squeezed out.

evidence.quote: Proposal 2 also leaves Agent_3 with no items.

mechanisms.theme: Final unanimous A1 gave last position a credible payoff above first
mechanisms.explanation: By Round 3 all agents proposed and accepted the same Track A1 allocation. Agent_1 accepted a discounted 20.25 utility, while Agent_3 accepted Stone+Quill worth 25.11 discounted, producing the first-less-than-last outcome.

evidence.quote: totals of 29 for Agent_1, 40 for Agent_2, and 31 for Agent_3
